parentQuestion: A 62-year-old patient with osteoarthritis shows knee cartilage breaking down faster than it can be repaired. Which cells have FAILED in their regenerative function?

scaffolds.q: Cartilage is a connective tissue that cushions joints.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Cartilage covers the ends of bones in joints, providing smooth surfaces and shock absorption.
Correct! From Greek. Chondrocyte = cartilage cell. Chondroblast = cartilage builder.

scaffolds.q: CHONDROCYTES are the cells that live in and maintain cartilage.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Chondrocytes secrete the collagen and proteoglycans that make up cartilage matrix.

scaffolds.q: Cartilage is AVASCULAR - it has no blood vessels.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Nutrients reach chondrocytes only by diffusion. No express delivery, just slow diffusion.

scaffolds.q: Osteoarthritis is a disease where cartilage breaks down.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! The protective cartilage wears away, eventually leading to painful bone-on-bone contact.

scaffolds.q: When cartilage breaks down faster than chondrocytes can repair it, osteoarthritis progresses.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The chondrocytes try to rebuild, but can't keep up with the damage. Slow healing tissue fighting a losing battle.

scaffolds.q: Osteocytes are BONE cells, not cartilage cells.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Osteo- = bone. Chondro- = cartilage. Different tissues, different cell names.

scaffolds.q: In osteoarthritis, CHONDROCYTES are failing to regenerate cartilage fast enough.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The chondrocytes are overwhelmed - breakdown exceeds their repair capacity, especially in avascular tissue.

scaffolds.q: The answer is CHONDROCYTES - the cartilage cells that can't keep up with repair.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Not fibroblasts (fibrous tissue), not osteoblasts (bone), not adipocytes (fat). Chondrocytes maintain cartilage.
